paragraphs.body: Once again, Drew sat on the cold marble counter with her feet in the sink and her knees to her chest. She leaned close to the mirror, staring at herself, illuminated by the harsh white bathroom light. Every day, she worked herself up about her appearance, unable to think about anything else besides people’s perception of her. She was completely consumed by self-consciousness and low self-esteem. When it got really bad, when these thoughts became debilitating, she would lock herself in her bathroom and obsess over her face and body for hours on end.

annotations.comment: I like the title (although it also made me worried!) and was immediately interested in the opening.

annotations.text: She was completely consumed by self-consciousness and low self-esteem.
annotations.comment: I wonder if you can let the scene show us this instead of so directly telling us? I feel like it is apparent from the scene.

paragraphs.body: Earlier in the day, when she was out shopping, Drew caught a glimpse of her reflection in a boutique’s window. She was caught off guard and her appearance made her instantly feel embarrassed and gross and uncomfortable. Her skin looked bad, her posture looked terrible, her body looked humiliating, her nose looked weird. She quickly moved away from the window but the uncomfortable feeling lingered. She hurried home because she couldn’t calm down the thoughts running through her mind and the physiological reaction she was having to them. She was acutely aware of the way her body felt and looked, like the weight of her nose on her face, the dryness of her skin, the grease in her hair. She needed to go home and knew what she had to do to calm down.

annotations.comment: Ugh, I relate to this. I bet lots of us will.
annotations.context_left: lier in the day, when she was out shopping, Drew caught a glimpse of her reflection in a boutique’s
annotations.context_right: . She was caught off guard and her appearance made her instantly feel embarrassed and gross and unco

annotations.text: She hurried home because she couldn’t calm down the thoughts running through her mind and the physiological reaction she was having to them. She was acutely aware of the way her body felt and looked, like the weight of her nose on her face, the dryness of her skin, the grease in her hair. She needed to go home and knew what she had to do to calm down.
annotations.comment: You can streamline this a bit. And again, not to beat this drum too hard, but the scene here does the work that your narrator told us!

paragraphs.body: Now, inches away from the mirror, directly under the severe lighting, Drew was able to fixate on each pore on her face. She pulled down on both of her temples, stretching the skin on her forehead. Like this, she could see how bumpy the skin was. She zeroed in on a particularly large bump in the center of her forehead. She used her forefingers to push on the blemish, her sharp acrylic nails making excellent tools for skin picking. She dug her nails into her skin, turning the blemish red and angry until it finally popped, leaking pus. She kept pushing after the initial release of pus until blood poured out of the small wound. She inched up close to the mirror and she picked at more bumps until her forehead was covered in small cuts and drying blood. She stared into the mirror and felt disgusted with herself. She yelled in frustration and took her sharp nails to her forehead, aggressively scratching to further irritate her skin.

annotations.context_left: opped, leaking pus. She kept pushing after the initial release of pus until blood poured out of the
annotations.context_right: wound. She inched up close to the mirror and she picked at more bumps until her forehead was covere

annotations.text: She yelled in frustration and took her sharp nails to her forehead, aggressively scratching to further irritate her skin.
annotations.comment: Again, the order of this paragraph may need slight adjustment. I took the prior sentence as the end of the ordeal, but here she’s gone back to it. I also wondered a bit about how this was helping her “calm down” as stated earlier. Like, I get it — I see what she’s doing. But I’m not sure it’s calming.

paragraphs.body: Finally satisfied with her work, Drew moved to her chin, sucking in her bottom lip to stretch the skin there. She got to work pushing and poking at the bumps here with her nails until the pain became unbearable. She abruptly pulled her hands away and leaned back from the mirror. She heaved, realizing she had been holding her breath as she concentrated on picking. She had been particularly harsh on one blemish near her lip and it bled profusely, dripping down Drew’s chin and onto her neck.

As she peered into the mirror, Drew ignored the awful state she left her skin in, instead focusing on her nose. She hated her nose more than anything. More than her skin, more than her body, more than her teeth, more than her voice, more than her personality, Drew couldn’t stand her nose. She often fantasized about cutting it clean off, exposing the muscles underneath, or breaking it over and over again, to keep it permanently bandaged and hidden from other people’s eyes, as well as her own. During her compulsory staring contests with herself, Drew avoided looking at her nose to prevent herself from completely spiraling. This time, though, she couldn’t look away. She imagined digging her nails into both sides of her nose, pushing at it until it too popped and leaked pus and blood, leaving behind a small gash that would quickly scar over like the other imperfections on her face that she messed with.

annotations.context_left: ged and hidden from other people’s eyes, as well as her own. During her compulsory staring contests
annotations.context_right: herself, Drew avoided looking at her nose to prevent herself from completely spiraling. This time,

paragraphs.body: She leaned forward until she was right against the mirror. She pulled up the tip of her nose, inspecting her nostrils. She pushed the tip from side to side, staring deeply into her pores. She used the bottom of her palm to push against the side of her nose as hard as she possibly could, her wrist shaking under the pressure. She abruptly pulled her hand away, closed it into a fist, and slammed it back into the side of her nose. She moved in close to the mirror, so her nose touched it and stared. Drew watched as tears welled up in her eyes and tracked them as they began to roll down her cheeks.

annotations.comment: Could you work in a sense of whether this particular behavior is typical for her or not? I get that this is a sort of ritual for her, but am not clear if this is an escalation or what she always does.

paragraphs.body: She slowly lifted her feet out of the sink and swiveled around to face away from the mirror. She hopped off the counter and wanted more than anything to just stop, leave the bathroom, and pretend she hadn’t done all this. She knew she was being crazy but there was truly no escaping this headspace when Drew became this wound up. She turned back around, looked up at herself, and punched her nose again.

annotations.comment: Definitely not “calming down”! I like the escalation, as disturbing as it is, but I do think we need more of a sense of what’s driving this *right now* — clearly it’s body dysmorphia and you’ve captured this so well. But what else is driving her beyond that glimpse of herself in the mirror? Was she feeling good about herself and it was ruined? Etc?

paragraphs.body: She sobbed and shook in pain as she tried over and over again to punch herself as hard as she could. Drew figured it was physically impossible to hit herself as hard as she desired. She leaned her arms on the counter with her head down and her eyes closed, refusing to look at herself. She already knew her face was puffy, red, and wet with tears. She felt something drip onto her hand and opened her eyes to see drops of bright red blood falling onto the counter. She looked up into the mirror at her bloody nose. Unable to stop herself, Drew leaned forward, glaring at her nose. She felt something stir in her, something more extreme than the usual self-loathing that accompanied these self-obsession episodes. Despite the pain and exhaustion she was experiencing, she felt a wave of energy. Red hot energy, fueled by desperation.

Drew took two steps back from the counter and felt dizzied by her racing thoughts. She knew this was irrational, but no matter what else she tried to think about she just couldn’t shake this thought. She had never felt a compulsion like this, so strong her vision blurred and her breathing slowed. She ripped her eyes away from the mirror and down to the marble counter, centering her gaze on the sharp corner. She felt her body start to give in to the impulse before her mind could give it a second thought.

With her eyes locked on the corner, Drew took aggressive steps forward and thrashed her head against the counter. Her nose perfectly slammed into the sharp edge, instantly breaking. She fell to her knees and blood gushed from her face onto the bathroom floor. She screamed and sobbed in anguish; she was hyperventilating, desperately trying to catch her breath as she felt blood pooling in her mouth; she was shaking and shivering, but reached up and gripped the counter. It was slicked with blood but she held on tight and managed to pull herself up, enough to look in the mirror.

annotations.comment: I saw this coming and was still upset by it.

paragraphs.body: Drew stared at her reflection. Her face and chest were covered in blood, which continued to flood out of her nose. Her breathing became more laborious and she coughed, spewing blood all over the mirror. The coughing seemed to make the blood rush out of her face faster. She became incredibly lightheaded and lost her grip on the counter, slipping back onto the bathroom floor. The back of her head crashed into the tile and she screamed out but was muffled by the blood in her mouth.

On her back, she could barely breathe. Her vision went in and out of focus like she was fighting sleep. She knew she needed to get off the floor but she couldn’t concentrate on the thought. She could only think about what she looked like right now. She couldn’t fathom what someone would think if they saw her like this. As the disgust and shame ate away at her, Drew curled in on herself, spitting up more blood in the process. She moaned in pain, then felt embarrassed by the sound. She felt an intense desire to pick at her face again to settle the shame she was experiencing. But it was fruitless. She went too far this time and still wasn’t satisfied. No amount of picking, pulling, or punching could ever subdue her neurosis.

annotations.text: think if they saw her like this.
annotations.comment: I think it would be helpful to know who she worries might find her.

Enzo, I enjoyed (perhaps the wrong word?) this draft. It’s extremely vivid and very upsetting but it a highly well-written way. I definitely had a sense of kinship and then felt myself pulling away from the story — like, oh, I wouldn’t do THAT. At around the point of the nose breaking, the story starts to veer into almost gonzo territory, where I had trouble buying this character as real (I completely did until that point) and I think that’s because of the intense focus on her in the bathroom. I’d like to have more of a sense of her life beyond those moments. To be clear, I don’t necessarily want a pat connection (her boyfriend is mean to her and she does this…) but a sense of the stakes and situation a bit more just to help me understand what’s going on. I also wonder if your plot might benefit from a reversal, a moment when we think she’s done or is going to let herself off the hook, but then doubles down? Finally, I wanted to ask you about sharing this with the class. I think it would have to go out with a trigger warning for violence, which I don’t mind doing at all and have done before. But sometimes authors would prefer not to workshop their work rather than put a content warning on it. It’s your call!
